title: Lemon baked cheesecake
subtitle: Set cream cheese, sharp lemon, biscuit base
excerpt: A baked cheesecake with a clean lemon flavour that cuts through the richness of the cream cheese filling. The biscuit base is pressed firm, the filling is smooth and properly set: not airy, not wet, and the top is creamy white with a pale gold edge.
sourceNotes: Baked cheesecake in the American tradition, adapted for UK ingredients (full-fat cream cheese, digestive biscuit base). The lemon variation follows a well-established British baking pattern documented in recipe collections from the 1970s onward. No single public-domain source; the ratios are synthesised from standard cream-cheese-to-egg proportions.

content.text: Two things produce a smooth, crack-free baked cheesecake: low heat and a

. The water bath is optional; many home bakers skip it and accept the occasional crack, but it consistently produces a more even, creamy result. The filling should be slightly wobbly in the centre when you remove it from the oven; it will set firm in the fridge.

Crush the digestive biscuits to fine crumbs in a food processor or in a bag with a rolling pin. Mix with the melted butter until the crumbs are evenly coated. Press firmly into the base of a greased 23 cm springform tin, using the back of a spoon to compact them evenly. Refrigerate for 30 minutes while you make the filling.

Preheat the oven to 160°C conventional (140°C fan). Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; an oven thermometer is worth the few minutes it takes to check, particularly for cheesecake. Beat the cream cheese and sugar together until smooth and lump-free. Add the soured cream, lemon zest, lemon juice, and vanilla and beat again. Add the eggs one at a time, mixing on low speed just until each one disappears, overbeating introduces air, which causes the cheesecake to crack as it settles. Fold in the flour.

Wrap the outside of the springform tin tightly in two layers of foil. Pour the filling over the chilled base. Place the tin in a deep roasting tin and add enough boiling water to come 2 cm up the sides. Bake for 50 to 55 minutes. The edges should be set and the centre should still have a slight wobble, like a barely-set jelly, when you nudge the tin. Turn the oven off and leave the cheesecake inside with the door slightly ajar for 30 minutes. This gradual cooling reduces cracking.

Remove from the water bath, run a thin knife around the inside edge of the tin (this releases any adhesion that would pull the surface as the cheesecake contracts), and cool to room temperature. Cover and refrigerate for at least 6 hours, or overnight. The texture is much better the following day.
